title: Distress ink background technique
subtitle: Building a layered watercolour-wash background on scrapbook pages using distress inks
excerpt: Distress inks are dye-based, water-reactive inks applied to a background page using an ink pad and blending tool. The technique relies on blending wet ink directly on the page and activating the ink with water spray to create a soft watercolour-wash effect. Layering two or three colours produces the tonal variation that distinguishes a built-up background from a flat colour.
sourceNotes: Distress ink backgrounds are a contemporary scrapbooking and cardmaking technique developed in the early 2000s. No pre-1928 source covers the technique. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dye-based, water-reactive ink that re-wets when moisture is applied. Used in layers on scrapbook pages to create aged, soft, or muted colour backgrounds.
A foam-tipped applicator used to pick up ink from a pad and apply it directly to a page surface in circular or sweeping strokes.

content.text: background starts as a dry application and is then water-activated to blend and soften the edges. The water-reactive property is the key: the ink can be pushed, pooled, and blended after it has been applied to the page, as long as the re-wetting happens before the ink fully dries.
Card stock or heavy scrapbook paper (180 gsm or heavier) as the background sheet. Lighter paper buckles when wet ink is applied.
Two or three ink pad colours. Colours that are adjacent on the colour wheel blend smoothly; complementary colours (opposite on the wheel) produce muddier transitions.
per colour, or wash and dry the same tool between colours.
Press the foam tip of the blending tool onto the ink pad to load it. Apply the ink to the background page in circular motions, working outward from the area where the colour is strongest.
Apply the first colour across roughly a third of the page. Apply the second colour across the opposite third. Allow the middle section to overlap slightly.
Mist the inked page lightly with water. The ink will re-wet and bleed slightly, softening the borders between colours.
Allow to dry fully before mounting photos or die-cuts. Use a heat tool to accelerate drying if needed.

attrs.symptom: Colours blend to a muddy brown in the overlap zone
attrs.cause: Complementary colours (red and green, blue and orange) produce neutral or brown mixtures when blended wet.

content.text: Use analogous colours (blue and teal, rose and peach) or colours with a white or cream between them to avoid muddy mixing. Add a third neutral colour as a buffer in the overlap zone.
